cards.front: Map the major IPv6 address types to their prefix:
cards.back: Global Unicast (GUA): 2000::/3 — internet-routable. Unique Local (ULA): fc00::/7 (private use, RFC 4193). Link-Local: fe80::/10 — auto-assigned per interface, never routed. Multicast: ff00::/8. Loopback: ::1/128. Unspecified: ::/128.

cards.front: What is the standard IPv6 LAN subnet mask, and why?
cards.back: /64. Lower 64 bits = interface ID. SLAAC and EUI-64 require a 64-bit interface ID, so /64 is the de facto standard for any LAN. Smaller subnets break SLAAC; larger waste space.

cards.front: What is a typical ISP allocation size for an enterprise IPv6, and how do you carve subnets from it?
cards.back: Common: /48 to a site (16 bits = 65,536 /64 LANs). Or /56 to small site (8 bits = 256 /64 LANs). The 4th hex group (hextet) becomes the subnet identifier. Example: 2001:db8:abcd::/48 → /64 subnets like 2001:db8:abcd:0010::/64, 2001:db8:abcd:0020::/64, etc.

cards.front: What are link-local addresses (fe80::/10) used for?
cards.back: Mandatory on every IPv6-enabled interface. Used for OSPFv3, EIGRPv6, NDP, and any control-plane traffic between directly-connected hosts. Routers use link-local as next-hop in IPv6 routing tables. Generated automatically (EUI-64) or with `ipv6 enable`.

cards.front: Configure an IPv6 GUA on Gi0/0 with EUI-64 derivation.
cards.back:
```
interface gi0/0
 ipv6 address 2001:db8:1::/64 eui-64
 no shutdown
```
The lower 64 bits will be derived from the interface MAC using EUI-64. To assign a specific full address: `ipv6 address 2001:db8:1::1/64`.

cards.front: How does EUI-64 derive the interface ID from a 48-bit MAC?
cards.back: (1) Split MAC into two 24-bit halves. (2) Insert FFFE in the middle (so 6 bytes → 8 bytes). (3) Flip the 7th bit (universal/local bit) of the first byte. Example: MAC 0022.0033.4455 → 0222.00FF.FE33.4455 (note 00 → 02, flipping the U/L bit).

cards.front: Multicast — list 4 well-known IPv6 multicast addresses.
cards.back: ff02::1 — all nodes on the link. ff02::2 — all routers. ff02::5 — all OSPF routers. ff02::6 — all OSPF DRs. ff02::1:ffXX:XXXX — solicited-node multicast (used by NDP, derived from last 24 bits of unicast).

cards.front: What does U/L bit mean in a MAC address, and how does EUI-64 use it?
cards.back: U/L = Universal/Local bit, the 7th bit of the first byte. UAA (Universally Administered) means burned-in MAC, U/L=0. LAA (Locally Administered) means manually set, U/L=1. EUI-64 conversion FLIPS this bit when forming an IPv6 interface ID — so a burned-in MAC starting 02:xx becomes 00:xx in the resulting interface ID, and vice versa.

cards.front: Three sections of an IPv6 Global Unicast Address (GUA), and typical sizes?
cards.back: Global routing prefix: typically 48 bits (assigned by ISP/RIR). Subnet identifier: typically 16 bits (lets enterprise carve up to 65,536 subnets). Interface identifier: 64 bits (host portion, often EUI-64 derived). Total = 128 bits.

cards.front: Structure of an IPv6 Unique Local Address (ULA): prefix bits, global ID, subnet ID, interface ID?
cards.back: FC00::/7 range (in practice all begin with FD — FC00::/8 is reserved for centrally-managed assignment, never used). Format: 8 bits = FD, 40 bits = randomly-chosen Global ID, 16 bits = Subnet ID, 64 bits = Interface ID. The random Global ID makes ULA prefixes effectively unique even between merged organizations.

cards.front: IPv6 multicast scope hex prefixes: FF01, FF02, FF05, FF08, FF0E.
cards.back: FF01 = Interface-local (loopback only). FF02 = Link-local (single segment, never routed). FF05 = Site-local. FF08 = Organization-local. FF0E = Global (routable). The second hex digit of an FFxx address encodes the scope.

cards.front: List the IPv6 cast types (and the IPv6 address with no broadcast equivalent).
cards.back: Unicast: one-to-one. Multicast: one-to-many (FF00::/8). Anycast: one-to-one-of-many — same address on multiple devices, the network routes to the topologically nearest. NO broadcast in IPv6 — anything that needed broadcast in IPv4 (ARP, DHCP discovery) uses multicast in IPv6.

cards.front: IPv6 unspecified (::), loopback (::1), and the 224.0.0.x ↔ FF02::x equivalents for routing protocols.
cards.back: Unspecified: :: (used as source when no address yet). Loopback: ::1 (= 127.0.0.1). All-nodes link-local IPv6: FF02::1 (= 224.0.0.1). All-routers IPv6: FF02::2 (= 224.0.0.2). OSPF: FF02::5 (=224.0.0.5), DR/BDR FF02::6 (=224.0.0.6). RIP: FF02::9 (=224.0.0.9). EIGRP: FF02::A (=224.0.0.10).
